coredata.dc:title: Combined treatment approaches based on ultrasound for removal of triazophos from wastewater
coredata.dc:creator: Jawale, Rajashree H.
Gogate, Parag R.
coredata.dc:description: Pesticides have been the major contributors to the growth of agricultural productivity, but the wide spread use in the fields and discharge from the manufacturing industries have also contributed to environmental concerns. In the present work, degradation of triazophos (O,O-diethyl-O-(1-phenyl-1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl) phosphorothioate) as a model pollutant has been investigated using high volume continuous ultrasonic flow cell for the first time. Effect of power dissipation and initial pH on the extent of triazophos degradation using acoustic cavitation has been investigated initially. Under the optimized set of operating power dissipation and pH, effect of addition of hydrogen peroxide (ratio of C12H16N3O3PS (Triazophos):H2O2 over the range of 1:1–1:5), ozone (over the flow rate of 100–400mg/h) and Fenton’s reagent (C12H16N3O3PS:FeSO4:H2O2 ratio over the range of 1:1:1–1:4:4) has been investigated as possible process intensification strategy. Combined operation of US with H2O2 and Ozone resulted in 48.6% and 54.6% triazophos degradation respectively whereas combination of US and Fenton’s reagent resulted in maximum degradation as 92.2% and also resulted in maximum COD removal as 88.5%. The study also focused on identification of intermediate products formed during the degradation as well as establishing the kinetic rate constants and the synergistic index for different approaches. The study has established that cavitation can be effectively used for triazophos degradation with significant intensification benefits based on the use of combination approach.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triazophos
Ultrasound
Flow cell
Combined approach
Additives
Intensification

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

Effect of operating power on the extent of degradation.
Effect of flow rate on the extent of degradation.
Effect of operating pH on the extent of degradation.
Estimation of kinetic rate constant at different pH.
Effect of loadings of H2O2on the extent of degradation for combined approach of US+H2O2.
Effect of flow rate of Ozone on the extent of degradation for combined approach of US+Ozone.
Effect of loadings of Fenton’s reagent on the extent of degradation for combined approach of US+Fenton’s reagent.
Kinetic rate constants at different power dissipation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Value in bracket gives the actual power dissipation measured calorimetrically.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Kinetic rate constants at different flow rate.
Kinetic rate constants at different pH.
Kinetic rate constants at different loadings of H2O2in the combined approach of US+H2O.
Kinetic rate constants at different flow rate of Ozone in the combined approach of US+Ozone.
Kinetic rate constants at different loadings of Fenton’s reagent in the combined approach of US+Fenton’s reagent.

row.entry: Pseudo First order Rate Constant k×103 (min−1)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: COD reduction for different approaches based on US.

head.ce:title: Combined treatment approaches based on ultrasound for removal of triazophos from wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering Department, Institute of Chemical Technology, Matunga, Mumbai 40019, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pesticides have been the major contributors to the growth of agricultural productivity, but the wide spread use in the fields and discharge from the manufacturing industries have also contributed to environmental concerns. In the present work, degradation of triazophos (O,O-diethyl-O-(1-phenyl-1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl) phosphorothioate) as a model pollutant has been investigated using high volume continuous ultrasonic flow cell for the first time. Effect of power dissipation and initial pH on the extent of triazophos degradation using acoustic cavitation has been investigated initially. Under the optimized set of operating power dissipation and pH, effect of addition of hydrogen peroxide (ratio of C12H16N3O3PS (Triazophos):H2O2 over the range of 1:1–1:5), ozone (over the flow rate of 100–400mg/h) and Fenton’s reagent (C12H16N3O3PS:FeSO4:H2O2 ratio over the range of 1:1:1–1:4:4) has been investigated as possible process intensification strategy. Combined operation of US with H2O2 and Ozone resulted in 48.6% and 54.6% triazophos degradation respectively whereas combination of US and Fenton’s reagent resulted in maximum degradation as 92.2% and also resulted in maximum COD removal as 88.5%. The study also focused on identification of intermediate products formed during the degradation as well as establishing the kinetic rate constants and the synergistic index for different approaches. The study has established that cavitation can be effectively used for triazophos degradation with significant intensification benefits based on the use of combination approach.

ce:section.ce:para: Necessity of using pesticides in agriculture is escalating every day especially with an objective of increasing the agricultural productivity to meet the required food demand based on the exponentially increasing population of the world. The usage of pesticides started in 1940’s to increase the yield of the plant as well as to control the weeds and pest growth on the cultivated plants. Currently, there are more than 500 different registered active substances used as pesticides annually. Excess usage of pesticides in the fields and possible discharge from the units has led to increasing pollution incidences in the fresh water system [1]. Majority of pesticides are carcinogenic and hence are potential health hazard for the community [2]. Typically pesticides are non-degradable recalcitrant complexes with significantly high half-life and hence there is a high chance of these chemicals mixing with water bodies from fields and significant quantum can also accumulate in soil. Pesticide industries also produce large amount of wastewater containing toxic and bio refractory compounds as the efficacy of traditionally used methods is limited and the overall pesticide pollution problem is significantly affecting human wellbeing and environment [3]. The issues related to contamination of water sources by the pesticides is of great concern especially in the agriculture dominated countries like India. The conventional wastewater treatment approaches also suffer from limitations such as high treatment time, formation of mutagenic compounds and potential production of various secondary wastes requiring further treatment before discharge. It is thus imperative to develop more effective novel wastewater treatment schemes for the removal of toxic chemicals such as pesticides.

The overview of application of different oxidation processes for triazophos degradation has been presented now to establish the novelty of the present work. Yang et al. [7] investigated the triazophos degradation using biological oxidation based on the use of Diaphorobacter sp. TPD-1 specially isolated from a contaminated soil containing triazophos in dominance. A biochemical pathway for degradation of triazophos was proposed in the work and Diaphorobacter sp. TPD-1 was reported to completely degrade 50mgL−1 triazophos and 1-phenyl-3-hydroxy-1,2,4-triazole (PHT) to non-detectable levels in 24 and 56h, respectively. Aungpradit and coworkers [8] studied the triazophos degradation using photocatalytic oxidation based on the simulated solar radiation and aqueous TiO2 suspension. The study proposed the transformation routes for the triazophos based on the identification of seventeen degradation products. Treatment of synthesized wastewater containing triazophos using Fenton’s reagent at bench scale was investigated by Li and coworkers [9], who reported that under optimum treatment conditions of pH as 4 and Fenton’s reagent loading (FeSO4·7H2O as 2.5g/L and H2O2 as 100mL/L), 96.3% degradation was obtained in 90min of treatment time with the residual COD concentration being 120mg/L. Similarly, Gogate and Patil [10] investigated the application of combined approach of hydrodynamic cavitation and AOPs for triazophos degradation. It was reported that the combination of hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) with ozone was the best treatment approach giving complete degradation whereas the combination of HC with Fenton’s reagent and only HC resulted in 80% and 50% degradation respectively. An analysis of the literature presented here revealed that investigations related to triazophos degradation using ultrasound either operated alone or in combination are lacking and hence the current work focused on the investigation using high volume ultrasonic continuous flow cell. It is also important to understand that most of the ultrasound related applications for degradation of pollutants have been using ultrasonic horn and bath which have poor scale prospects. Thus, the application of high volume ultrasonic flow cell for triazophos degradation forms the novelty of the present work. The objective of the study was to investigate the effect of important operating parameters and evaluate the efficacy of combined treatment approach based on ultrasound, hydrogen peroxide, ozone and Fenton’s reagent for degradation of triazophos. It is important to establish the effects of power dissipation or pH for specific compounds as the optimum values are likely to be dependent on the specific pollutants as well as reactor designs and capacity. Considering this requirement, the initial studies were directed to understand the effect of power dissipation and initial pH as well as the recirculation flow rate through the flow cell. Present work has also focused on the identification of intermediates as well as establishing kinetic rate constants and the synergistic index for degradation of triazophos.

Commercial grade triazophos (60% purity with other components being deltamethrin, acetic anhydride, ionic and non-ionic emulsifiers as per the information from supplier) was obtained from Cheminova India Limited, Panoli, Gujarat. The structure of triazophos has been given in Fig. 1 . Methanol used for HPLC as eluent was procured from J.T. Baker Chemical Pvt. Ltd. H2SO4 and NaOH used for adjustment of pH were procured from S.D. Fine Chem. Pvt. Ltd, Mumbai, India. All the solutions of required concentrations were prepared freshly in the laboratory using distilled water.

Sonics vibra-cell (continuous flow cell with capacity of 1.5L, frequency of 40kHz, rated power dissipation of 1500W) has been used in the present work as a source of ultrasonic irradiations. A pump has been used to circulate the solution through the reactor at the desired flow rate. A schematic representation of the continuous flow reactor has been given in Fig. 2 .

In the present work, an initial concentration of triazophos solution as 20mg/L has been used and kept constant for all the experiments based on the results presented in the earlier work [10] and also based on the generally observed concentration levels for the pesticide content in wastewater [2]. Degradation of triazophos has been investigated using 4L aqueous solution of triazophos for 90min of treatment time at 37°C as operating temperature to compare the efficacy of different approaches. Samples were withdrawn at regular intervals of time to analyze the progress of degradation. For investigating the effect of H2O2 loading, different ratios viz. 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, 1:5 (triazophos to
H
2
O
2
) have been used. Ozone generator (Model-DO400) having maximum operating flow rate as 400mg/h obtained from Eltech Engineering, Mumbai was used in the study to introduce ozone gas for the treatment. The effect of ozone flow rate was studied using different values as 100, 200, 300, and 400mg/h. Ozone was introduced into the triazophos solution using a bubble diffuser. Effect of Fenton’s reagent loading was investigated using different Triazophos: FeSO4:H2O2 ratios over the range of 1:1:1–1:2:4. All the experiments were repeated to check the reproducibility and the variations in the obtained data have been shown in the form of error bars in the figures.

Determination of triazophos concentration at any instant of time was achieved using High Pressure Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) unit obtained from Agilent Technologies, USA (1200 infinity series with main components as auto sampler, eclipse plus C-18 column with dimensions of 2.1×50mm, HPLC pump and UV detector). The mobile phase used for the analysis was mixture of methanol and de-ionized water (90:10 v/v) with a flow rate of 0.1mL/min. Triazophos determination wavelength was set at 243nm and the retention time was 1.6min. The progress of the treatment was also analyzed in terms of COD removal using the open reflux method based on the use of COD digester where the sample is refluxed for 2h in strong acid solution with excess potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7). The residual K2Cr2O7 is titrated with ferrous ammonium sulfate to establish the K2Cr2O7 consumed and hence the oxidizable matter equivalent.

Kinetic analysis of the triazophos degradation was based on the integral method. The plot of
ln
C
A 0
/
C
A
vs time (t) was observed to be a straight line passing through the origin, which confirmed that, the degradation of triazophos using ultrasonic flow cell followed a first order reaction kinetics. The intermediates formed during the degradation of triazophos were identified using the liquid chromatography–mass spectroscopy (LC–MS) analysis.

The effect of power dissipation on the triazophos degradation was studied by varying the amplitude over the range of 40–80%. The actual values of amplitude used with the power dissipation measured calorimetrically are 40% (actual power dissipation as 127.6W), 60% (164.4W), 70% (183.7W), 75% (185.7W) and 80% (203.6W). For this study of power dissipation, constant pH of 7.3 was used, which also correspond to natural pH. Power density is an important factor in deciding the cavitational activity and hence the degradation of organic pollutants [11,12]. The obtained results have been given in Fig.3 and Table 1 . The extent of degradation of triazophos was observed to increase significantly with an increase in the ultrasonic power over the entire range of power. Degradation of 35.4% was obtained at 80% amplitude, whereas only 11.9% degradation was observed at 40% amplitude after 90 min of treatment. Also, the kinetic rate constant increased from 2.3×10−3 to 4.8×10−3 min−1 for an increase in the amplitude from 40% to 80% respectively. The increase in triazophos degradation with an increase in ultrasonic power is attributed to the fact that the number of active cavitation bubbles increases with an increase in the ultrasonic power. Due to the increase in cavitational activity, the concentration of hydroxyl radicals generated is higher at higher power which leads to enhanced triazophos degradation. The enhanced production of radicals at higher power dissipation has been confirmed based on the salicylic acid dosimetry measurements as per the reports of Chakinala et al. [13]. An excessive increase in power dissipation can also lead to lower efficiency of energy transfer to solution as the coalescence of the cavities can occur at significantly higher power dissipation levels [14]. Since 1500 W is the rated power of this ultrasonic equipment, it is not recommended to use it at maximum power dissipation throughout the treatment. Due to these issues, the maximum ultrasonic power was fixed at 80% amplitude viz. 1200 W as the supplied power. It is observed that as amplitude increases from 40% to 70% degradation increases by 6% whereas when the amplitude increases from 70% to 80% degradation still increases by 8% and hence the optimum of 80% amplitude has been used in further experiments. Lan et al. [15] studied the effect of power on the extent of degradation of fuchsin basic and reported that the extent of degradation increased with increasing ultrasonic power. Degradation of 75.4% was reported at 500W power whereas only about 38.3% degradation was observed at 200W after 60min irradiation. The degradation of fuchsin basic in aqueous solution under stirring condition (without sonication) was examined as a control experiment and it was reported that in the absence of ultrasound, the degradation did not take place whereas rapid degradation of fuchsin basic was obtained in the presence of ultrasound. Wu et al. [16] studied the influence of ultrasonic power on phenol degradation and reported that the degradation rate of phenol in water samples was maximum at optimum ultrasonic output power of 200W. Degradation of Rhodamine B under the influence of ultrasound at varying power was investigated by Sivakumar and Pandit [17] and it was reported that increased degradation is observed only till optimum, beyond which the extent of degradation decreased. The existence of the optimum was attributed to the formation of larger bubble clouds and the induced decoupling effect. It is important to note that optimum values are not the same for all the ultrasonic equipment and in the current work; no optimum was observed making the study of the effect of power dissipation on the degradation of triazophos or for that matter any pollutant important.

Experiments related to effect of flow rate were performed at different flow rates (245, 360, 480, 735, 1000mL/min) with same initial concentration of 20ppm using 80% amplitude at natural solution pH of 7.3 and the obtained results have been depicted in Fig. 4 . It was observed that for same power input the extent of degradation increased with an increase in the flow rate till 480mL /min and further increase in the flow rate resulted in lower extent of degradation. In 90min of irradiation time, 35.4% extent of degradation was observed at 480mL/min which was higher compared to 12.2% degradation obtained at 245mL/min of flow rate. Table 2 gives the obtained results for the extent of degradation at various flow rates. The experimental system considered in the work involves a closed loop circulation of 4L of triazophos solution for 90min of treatment time where the recirculation rate directly decides the number of passes or cycles that the solution will be exposed to irradiation. On the other hand the actual residence time of the solution in one cycle in the irradiation zone decreases with an increase in the circulation flow rate. An increase in the flow rate results in a increase in the cavitational activity up to optimum flow rate of 480mL/min giving higher extent of degradation. Beyond the optimum, a high recycle rate contributes significantly to the non-uniformity of the cavitational activity resulting into detrimental effects. Though not many reports were observed for the case of degradation using ultrasonic reactors, credence to this hypothesis can be obtained based on similar studies for the cavitational reactors in general. Loranger et al. [18] studied the effect of recirculation rate on the dispersion quality of 2L oxidized pulp (2% consistency, a rotor-stator gap of 0.042mm and pH 7) for a treatment time of 1h. It was reported that flow rates between 200 and 800mL/min with few number of passes and high retention time gave better gel quality whereas at higher flow rates (beyond 800mL/min) dispersion quality reduced drastically. Gohil and Saini [19] also investigated effect of velocity on cavitation rate in a Francis turbine and reported the existence of optimum velocity as 3.34m/s for maximum benefits. Similar results for the ultrasound assisted depolymerization of aqueous solution of poly (vinyl alcohol) in aqueous solution have been reported by Harkal et al. [20].

Physical and chemical properties of the solution are strongly affected by pH and hence pH plays a major role in deciding the extent of degradation. For understanding the effect of pH, experiments were performed at different pH values over the range of 2.3 to 7.3 with 20ppm triazophos solution at 80% amplitude for 90min. The degradation was higher at lower pH values as per the data depicted in Fig.5 (a) and the maximum degradation of 43% was obtained at pH of 3.2. Fig.5 (b) shows the pseudo first order reaction kinetics plot of ln(CA0/CA) vs time (t) and the obtained values of rate constants have been given in Table 3 . It was observed that the kinetic rate constant decreased from 19.4×10−3 to 4.8×10−3min−1for an increase in the pH from 3.2 to 7.3. The observed better results for the lower pH values can be attributed to the easier generation of hydroxyl radicals (OH) and higher oxidation potential. Also, the molecular form of triazophos (pKa=3.95) is dominant under strongly acidic conditions which drives the higher concentration of the pollutant at the site of cavity collapse and hence higher extent of degradation occurs based on pyrolysis and free radical attack. If the pH is higher than the pKa value, the ionic form predominates, which cannot diffuse into reactive zones and hence lower extent of degradation is obtained. Based on the results of experiments at various pH, further experiments were performed at pH of 3.2, as maximum degradation is obtained at this pH.

Jawale et al. [21] reported enhanced degradation of potassium ferrocyanide at lower pH whereas Kotronarou et al. [22] reported that decomposition of aqueous solution of parathion using ultrasonic irradiation was favored by a decrease in the pH. Mishra and Gogate [23] also reported that maximum degradation was obtained at pH of 2.5 and the extent of degradation of p-nitrophenol decreased with an increase in the pH. It is important to note that though the nature of the trends is similar, the increase in the extent of pollutant degradation with changes in pH is different. In some cases only marginal increase is observed which means that pH adjustment may not be feasible considering the final discharge norms and issues with readjustment of the pH before final discharge. In the present work, extent of degradation increased by only about 7.6% as pH decreased from 7.3 to 3.2 and it can be said that using natural pH of solution will be better as it also matches the final discharge criteria. The understanding into the pKa value, the natural pH of the solution and the extent of increase in the degradation based on pH adjustment is important to decide the recommended pH value for maximum benefits.

Ultrasound induces the dissociation of hydrogen peroxide giving hydroxyl radicals and hence, H2O2 can be used to enhance the extent of degradation. In the present work, ultrasound combined with
H
2
O
2
was applied for the degradation of 20ppm triazophos solution at different
H
2
O
2
loading expressed as ratio of triazophos to
H
2
O
2
as 1:2, 1:3, 1:4 and 1:5. The total quantity of
H
2
O
2
has been added as single injection at the start of the experiment. The obtained results have been shown in Fig. 6 . The extent of degradation was observed to increase with an increase in the hydrogen peroxide loading till an optimum ratio of 1:3. Beyond this optimum, a marginal decrease in the extent of degradation was observed for the ratio of 1:4 and subsequently, the extent of degradation significantly decreased at 1:5 ratio. Maximum degradation of 52.4% was obtained at 1:3 ratio of triazophos to
H
2
O
2
as depicted in Fig. 5. Table 4 established that the rate constant increased from 19.9×10−3 min−1 to 21.5×10−3 min−1 for an increase in the ratio of triazophos to
H
2
O
2
from 1:2 to an optimum of 1:3.

During the treatment, the scavenging of the OH
radicals is also taking place by H2O2 itself and also based on the recombination reaction of OH
radicals. Therefore, the effect of adding H2O2 will be dependent on the utilization of the generated OH
by the pollutant giving an optimum loading. It is also expected that for the specific pollutant in question, the optimum loading of H2O2 will be different and hence always needs to be established. Wu et al. [16] studied the phenol degradation with different H2O2 loadings for a treatment time of 2h and reported that 3mL/L H2O2loading was the optimum at which maximum of 31.1% degradation is obtained. Patil and Gogate [24] studied the dichlorvos degradation using ultrasound in the presence of different loadings ranging from dichlorvos: H2O2 ratio as 1:1 to 1:50 and established the existence of optimum pollutant to oxidant ratio as 1:25.

The comparison of US, H2O2 and US+H2O2 processes has been established in terms of synergistic index (f) for the combined process which was calculated as follows:
(1)
f =
k
US /
H
2
O
2
k
US
+
k
H
2
O
2

The kinetic rate constant of 0.9×10−3 min−1 for the degradation using only H2O2 (without US) significantly increased to 24.7×10−3 min−1 when H2O2 was combined with ultrasound confirming synergy. Observed synergistic index of 1.4 for the combination of US and H2O2 in the present work is attributed to increased rate of dissociation of H2O2 in the presence of ultrasound yielding more OH
radicals, which are also effectively utilized by triazophos at significant rate. Merouani et al. [25] also reported that the combination of hydrogen peroxide and ultrasound resulted in enhanced degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) with optimum loading established as 100mg/L. Also, it was reported that a similar loading of H2O2 in the absence of ultrasound resulted in negligible degradation of RhB even after 240min. From literature and obtained results in present work, it can be established that optimum H2O2 loading is different for different pollutants under consideration with different degree of intensification and hence it is very important to perform study for specific pollutant, clearly establishing the importance of the present study on understanding the effect of hydrogen peroxide loading.

Use of ozone can be an effective approach for the removal of different pollutants like pesticides and dyes. Ozonation combined with ultrasonic irradiations results in generation of enhanced quantum of hydroxyl radicals and this combination method can be more beneficial for degradation of pesticides due to existence of dual oxidation mechanisms and better utilization of introduced ozone [26]. In the present work, ultrasound combined with ozone was applied for the degradation of 20ppm triazophos solution at different ozone flow rates viz. 100, 200, 300 and 400mg/h. From the results depicted in Fig. 7 , it can be observed that the extent of degradation of triazophos increased with an increase in the ozone flow rate used in the combination approach. At optimized operating parameters, approach of only ultrasound gives 43% degradation and combination of ultrasound with ozone (flowrate 400mg/h) resulted in 52.4% triazophos degradation. Also initial kinetic rate constant increased from 22.2×10−3 min−1 in the absence of ozone to 28.8×10−3 min−1 for the operation involving combination of ultrasound with ozone as depicted in Table 5 . Use of only ozone resulted in only marginal extent of degradation. Synergetic effect has been observed when ozone was combined with ultrasound since the value of kinetic rate constant of 0.5×10−3 min−1 for the degradation process using only Ozone (without US) significantly increased to 29.3×10−3 min−1 when ozone was combined with ultrasound. Combined operation of US and ozone has been found to yield synergistic index of 1.5.This enhanced degradation of triazophos obtained in the combined operation is attributed to the formation of additional oxidants as OH
radicals and nascent oxygen due to the decomposition of ozone induced by cavitation. Also, enhanced mass transfer due to turbulence effects gives better utilization of ozone leading to higher extent of degradation. Gogate and Patil [10] have also reported similar results for the combination of ozonation (1.95g/h) with hydrodynamic cavitation and it was also established that intensification of the process depends on ozone flow rate and the location of injection of ozone for combined treatment approach. Yue et al. [27] also reported the synergetic effect of ozone and ultrasonic radiation for the chitosan degradation, also establishing that the mechanisms of ozone degradation of chitosan with and without ultrasound were entirely different. Patil and Gogate [24] reported that only ozonation resulted in 60.4% and 93.4% degradation of dichlorvos in 30min with 0.576g/h and 1.95g/h flow rate of ozone respectively, Also, ozonation combined with ultrasonic irradiations resulted in complete degradation of in 75 and 45min with 0.576g/h and 1.95g/h flow rate of ozone respectively.

The combined approach of acoustic cavitation with Fenton’s reagent was also investigated at different Triazophos: FeSO4:H2O2 ratios as 1:1:1, 1:1:2, 1:2:2 and 1:2:4. The obtained results have been shown in Fig. 8 and Table 6 . It can be seen that as the loading of FeSO4 and H2O2 increases, the extent of degradation increases. In 90min of treatment, minimum (1:1:1) and maximum (1:2:4) loading of Fenton’s reagent resulted in 62.7% and 92.2% triazophos degradation respectively. The kinetic analysis for this approach was based on the pseudo-first order mechanism being considered over the initial 20min treatment (where maximum extent of degradation has been obtained) as represented in Fig. 7. Table 6 gives the data for the kinetic rate constants at different loadings of Fenton’s reagent. It can be observed that an increase in the loading of Fenton’s reagent from 1:1:1 to 1:2:4 resulted in an increase in the rate constant from 36.4×10−3 min−1 to 116.2×10−3 min−1. From the data given in the Table 6, it can be said that maximum synergistic effect is observed with synergistic index of 4.42 since the value of kinetic rate constant of 8.8×10−3 min−1 for the degradation using Fenton’s reagent (without US) increased to 124.6×10−3 min−1 when Fenton’s reagent was combined with ultrasound. Joshi and Gogate [28] reported a similar enhancement in the dichlorvos degradation from 16% to 91.5% by combining hydrodynamic cavitation with Fenton’s reagent. The combination of ultrasound with the Fenton’s reagent results in the intensified production of OH
radicals based on the breakage of the complex yielding Fe2+ back in the chain of reactions as per the detailed mechanisms discussed in earlier literature reports [5,14]. Bagal et al. [29] have also reported the synergistic effects for the US/Fenton process (98.7% degradation) as compared to only ultrasonic treatment (5.8%) or only Fenton’s process (21%) for the removal of DNP. Similar trends for the triazophos degradation using combination of Fenton’s reagent with ultrasonic flow cell have been observed in the present work with 92.2% degradation for combined approach of US and Fenton’s reagent (1:4:4 loading) and only 43% degradation using individual approach of US.

The progress of the treatment has also been analyzed in terms of the extent of COD removal. The obtained results in terms of extent of COD reduction based on the use of acoustic cavitation generated by ultrasonic flow cell alone and different combinations have been given in Table 7 . It has been observed that use of ultrasound alone resulted in about 24.4% COD removal in 90min of treatment. Combination of ultrasound with H2O2 resulted in enhanced removal of COD (48.6%) attributed to the enhanced production of free radicals. Combination of ultrasound with ozone and Fenton’s reagent resulted in 54.6% and 88.5% COD removal respectively, which was significantly higher as compared to the use of ultrasound alone. Combination of ultrasound/ Fenton’s reagent was again established as the best treatment approach giving highest COD (88.5%) removal among all the combinations, also establishing the correspondence with the extents of degradation.

Intermediate analysis was performed for the best treatment approach of combination of ultrasound with Fenton chemistry at an optimum loading of Fenton’s reagent where maximum extent of degradation was obtained. The analysis was based on the use of liquid chromatography–mass spectroscopy (LC–MS). The compounds detected were 3Z Undecene-5,7,10-triynoicacid, 1-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-2-hydroxymethyl-5-nitroimidazole, Acetylisoniazid, Zolpidem Metabolite I, Dihydrodeoxystreptomycin, Swietenine, Crocetin (Fig. 9 ). It is important to note that Aniline, N-phenyl formamide, 3-hydroxy-1-phenyl-1,2,4-triazole, and triazophos oxon are common compounds which are observed for free radical based oxidation of triazophos. Based on the identification of the intermediates, it can said that the first step involved in the degradation of triazophos is the hydrolysis of the P–O ester bond of triazophos to form 1-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-2-hydroxymethyl-5-nitroimidazole as heterocyclic compound. Azole ring cleavage subsequently on hydrolysis produced crocetin [8].

It is also important to note that the initial pesticide solution contained triazophos (60% purity) as the main component and additionally deltamethrin, acetic anhydride, ionic and non ionic emulsifiers were also present which may have resulted in formation of other compounds such as 3Z Undecene-5,7,10-triynoicacid, Acetylisoniazid, Zolpidem Metabolite I, Dihydrodeoxystreptomycin and Swietenine.

The studies related to the degradation of triazophos using ultrasound alone and in combination with different oxidants has led to following important conclusions:
•
Ultrasonic flow cell can be effectively applied for the degradation of triazophos at relatively large operating capacity of 4L.
•
Acidic conditions with optimum pH as 3.2, higher power dissipation and optimum flow rate as 480mL/min are the best operating conditions for the ultrasound based approach.
•
Combination of ultrasound with H2O2 and Ozone results in enhanced extents of degradation. The combination of ultrasound with Fenton’s reagent (1:2:4 ratio) results in almost 90% degradation of triazophos in 30min of treatment time with 88.5% COD reduction and synergistic index of 4.42.
•
The treatment cost plays an important role in the selection of a suitable treatment process. Based on the actual electrical energy consumption and the treatment time required to get the maximum degradation, it can be said that cost of individual operation based on the use of ultrasound will be significantly higher as compared to combined treatment approach. Combination of ultrasound with Fenton’s reagent (1:2:4 ratio) will be the most cost effective approach among all the processes studied in present work since the reduction in reaction time is significant also yielding maximum degradation and COD reduction.

Overall, an optimized treatment scheme of combined processes based on ultrasound and Fenton chemistry is the best approach for effective removal of triazophos and the present work has shown the feasibility of such an approach at operating capacity of 4L.
